prompt: In a group of 23 randomly chosen people, what is the probability (in %) that at least two of them share a birthday?
source: Standard birthday-paradox derivation. See Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability, §3.1.
revealExplanation: 23 people make C(23, 2) = 253 distinct pairs, and each pair has roughly a 1/365 chance of matching. Most people anchor on ‘23 versus 365’ and miss that pair-multiplication, which is why the probability crosses 50% so much earlier than intuition suggests.
revealWorked: P(at least one match) = 1 − P(all distinct) = 1 − (365 × 364 × … × 343) / 365^23 ≈ 0.5073.
